quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Reversible reaction"?

options.text: π = iMRT; the pressure needed to prevent osmosis across a semipermeable membrane; used to determine molar mass of large molecules
ΔTf = iKfm; solute particles lower the freezing point by disrupting crystal formation
A measure of molecular polarity; vector sum of all bond dipoles; symmetric molecules (CO₂, CCl₄) can have polar bonds but no net dipole
A reaction that can proceed in both forward and reverse directions, denoted by the double arrow ⇌

quiz_questions.explanation: "Reversible reaction" is defined as: A reaction that can proceed in both forward and reverse directions, denoted by the double arrow ⇌. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Position of equilibrium"?

options.text: Temporary dipole-induced dipole attractions present in ALL molecules; strength increases with molar mass, polarizability, and surface area
The relative amounts of reactants and products at equilibrium; lies to the right (product-favored) when K > 1
The number of moles of compound that dissolve per liter of saturated solution; calculated from Ksp and stoichiometry
An unwanted reaction that competes with the desired reaction, consuming reactants and reducing the yield of the target product

quiz_questions.explanation: "Position of equilibrium" is defined as: The relative amounts of reactants and products at equilibrium; lies to the right (product-favored) when K > 1. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Closed system"?

options.text: Acid: produces H⁺(aq); base: produces OH⁻(aq); limited to aqueous solutions
A system that can exchange energy but not matter with the surroundings; required for true chemical equilibrium
Properties that depend on the number of dissolved particles, not their chemical identity: ΔTb, ΔTf, π, ΔP
-log[H⁺]; a logarithmic scale where each unit represents a 10-fold change in [H⁺]; pH 3 is 1000× more acidic than pH 6

quiz_questions.explanation: "Closed system" is defined as: A system that can exchange energy but not matter with the surroundings; required for true chemical equilibrium. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Equilibrium vs. completion"?

options.text: The number of possible arrangements of particles and energy; related to entropy by Boltzmann equation: S = k ln W
A balanced equation that includes the enthalpy change (ΔH); coefficients represent moles, and the physical state of each species must be specified
Relates free energy to cell potential; n = moles of electrons transferred, F = Faraday's constant (96,485 C/mol)
Most reactions are reversible and reach equilibrium; very few go to completion (irreversible) unless products are removed from the system

quiz_questions.explanation: "Equilibrium vs. completion" is defined as: Most reactions are reversible and reach equilibrium; very few go to completion (irreversible) unless products are removed from the system. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: In the context of Dynamic Equilibrium, which best defines "Dynamic equilibrium"?

options.text: A model stating reactions occur when molecules collide with sufficient energy (≥ Ea) and proper orientation
A balanced equation that includes the enthalpy change (ΔH); coefficients represent moles, and the physical state of each species must be specified
The state where forward and reverse reaction rates are equal; concentrations are constant but molecular-level reactions continue
A non-rechargeable battery; the cell reaction is irreversible or impractical to reverse (e.g., alkaline, zinc-carbon)

quiz_questions.explanation: In Dynamic Equilibrium, "Dynamic equilibrium" refers to: The state where forward and reverse reaction rates are equal; concentrations are constant but molecular-level reactions continue.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A reaction that can proceed in both forward and reverse directions, denoted by the double arrow ⇌"?

options.text: Aufbau principle
Intermediate
Hybridization
Reversible reaction

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Reversible reaction".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The relative amounts of reactants and products at equilibrium; lies to the right (product-favored) when K > 1"?

options.text: Decomposition reaction
Position of equilibrium
ΔS prediction rules
Lewis structure

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Position of equilibrium".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A system that can exchange energy but not matter with the surroundings; required for true chemical equilibrium"?

options.text: Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution
Crystalline solid
Average bond energy
Closed system

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Closed system".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Most reactions are reversible and reach equilibrium; very few go to completion (irreversible) unless products are removed from the system"?

options.text: pKa
Second law of thermodynamics
Standard free energy of formation
Equilibrium vs. completion

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Equilibrium vs. completion".

quiz_questions.question_text: In Dynamic Equilibrium, which term matches: "The state where forward and reverse reaction rates are equal; concentrations are constant but molecular-level reactions continue"?

options.text: Pressure/volume change
Diprotic acid
Dynamic equilibrium
Decomposition reaction

quiz_questions.explanation: In Dynamic Equilibrium, this refers to "Dynamic equilibrium".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Dynamic Equilibrium is accurate?

options.text: Q (reaction quotient) compared to K predicts the direction a reaction will shift.
Crystalline solids have ordered lattice structures (ionic, molecular, covalent network, metallic), while amorphous solids (glass) lack long-range order.
Dynamic Equilibrium is reached when a reversible reaction's forward and reverse rates become equal, with no net change in concentrations.
Adding reactant or removing product shifts equilibrium forward (toward products); adding product or removing reactant shifts it in reverse.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Dynamic Equilibrium as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Dynamic Equilibrium?

options.text: These trends explain reactivity patterns: alkali metals are highly reactive (low IE, lose e⁻ easily), while noble gases are inert (full valence shells, high IE).
Entropy increases when: solids melt or liquids vaporize, gases expand, temperature increases, or the number of particles increases.
At equilibrium, the system is NOT static—both reactions continue at equal rates (dynamic).
Mass number (A) = protons + neutrons.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Dynamic Equilibrium as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Dynamic Equilibrium?

options.text: The relative strength of an acid is inversely related to the strength of its conjugate base.
This allows calculation of ΔH for reactions that are difficult to measure directly, by combining known reactions.
Equilibrium can be approached from either direction and is characterized by constant macroscopic properties (concentrations, color, pressure).
Percent yield (actual/theoretical × 100%) measures reaction efficiency.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Dynamic Equilibrium as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Dynamic Equilibrium?

options.text: The half-equivalence point of a weak acid titration is where pH = pKa, useful for buffer preparation.
The choice of method depends on the properties of the mixture components and the purity required.
Equilibrium Constants quantify the position of equilibrium.
The time to reach equilibrium depends on kinetic factors, while the position of equilibrium depends on thermodynamic factors.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Dynamic Equilibrium as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Dynamic Equilibrium?

options.text: Lewis structures show bonding and lone pairs, with formal charge minimization guiding the best structure.
Rate Laws and Reaction Order describe the mathematical relationship between reaction rate and reactant concentrations.
Adding reactant or removing product shifts equilibrium forward (toward products); adding product or removing reactant shifts it in reverse.
Dynamic Equilibrium is reached when a reversible reaction's forward and reverse rates become equal, with no net change in concentrations.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Dynamic Equilibrium as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Dynamic Equilibrium?

options.text: Enzyme inhibitors as drugs: statins lower cholesterol by inhibiting HMG-CoA reductase, effectively raising the Ea for cholesterol synthesis
Greenhouse gas behavior: CO₂ (linear, no permanent dipole) still absorbs IR radiation through asymmetric stretching, while symmetric stretching is IR-inactive
Nitrogen dioxide equilibrium (2NO₂ ⇌ N₂O₄): the brown-to-colorless color change visually demonstrates equilibrium shifting with temperature changes
Industrial Haber process using iron catalyst to lower Ea for N₂ + 3H₂ → 2NH₃ from 230 kJ/mol (uncatalyzed) to ~60 kJ/mol, making ammonia synthesis economically viable

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Dynamic Equilibrium.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Dynamic Equilibrium?

options.text: Soda carbonation: CO₂(g) ⇌ CO₂(aq) equilibrium maintained under pressure; opening the bottle shifts equilibrium toward gas release (fizzing)
Steel alloys: adding carbon (interstitial) to iron increases hardness; adding chromium and nickel (substitutional) creates stainless steel resistant to corrosion
Antacid tablet surface area experiments: crushed tablets dissolve faster than whole tablets because increased surface area increases reaction rate
Combustion of hydrocarbons in engines: CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O releasing energy to power vehicles; incomplete combustion produces toxic CO

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Dynamic Equilibrium.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Dynamic Equilibrium applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Quality control in fuel production: measuring heat of combustion per gram to verify fuel grade and energy content
Hemoglobin-oxygen binding equilibrium shifting in lungs (high O₂, favoring HbO₂) vs. tissues (low O₂, favoring O₂ release)
Hydrogen fuel cell vehicles (Toyota Mirai): H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O generating electricity with only water as byproduct; zero emissions at point of use
Biological research: phosphate buffer (H₂PO₄⁻/HPO₄²⁻, pKa = 7.20) commonly used in cell culture media to maintain physiological pH

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Dynamic Equilibrium.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Dynamic Equilibrium?

options.text: Industrial process design using Hess's Law to predict energy requirements for multi-step chemical syntheses without performing every reaction experimentally
Combustion of hydrocarbons in engines: CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O releasing energy to power vehicles; incomplete combustion produces toxic CO
Aluminum production by Hall-Héroult process: electrolysis of molten Al₂O₃ in cryolite at ~960°C; consumes ~5% of US electricity production
Nitrogen dioxide equilibrium (2NO₂ ⇌ N₂O₄): the brown-to-colorless color change visually demonstrates equilibrium shifting with temperature changes

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Dynamic Equilibrium.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Dynamic Equilibrium in practice?

options.text: Electric vehicle batteries: Tesla uses lithium-ion cells (LiNiCoAlO₂ cathode) delivering ~250 Wh/kg, enabling 300+ mile ranges
Soda carbonation: CO₂(g) ⇌ CO₂(aq) equilibrium maintained under pressure; opening the bottle shifts equilibrium toward gas release (fizzing)
Lithium-ion batteries: rechargeable cells using Li⁺ intercalation in graphite (anode) and metal oxide (cathode); powering smartphones, laptops, and electric vehicles
Corrosion of iron (rusting): 4Fe + 3O₂ + 6H₂O → 4Fe(OH)₃ → 2Fe₂O₃·3H₂O; prevented by galvanizing (Zn coating) or painting

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Dynamic Equilibrium.
